speaker: interviewer
text: Helen Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 2 with Helen Mark. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came back to Terezin. [SIGHS] And we had a good time those two months. But then came again the [SIGHS] not-so-nice times. And something happened in my life which was very, very sad. And that was that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] one day, a friend of my family who was in Terezin was arrested by the Gestapo, who came from Prague, picked him up, and took him back to Prague. And his brother was also in Terezin. And I knew them very well. And two days later, I walked towards my office. And I saw on the street the man who had been taken to Prague. And I was so happy. And I ran towards him and called, Emil, Emil. And he took one look at me, turned around, and ran away. And I just couldn't figure out what happened. And so I went to his brother's office. And I said, I just saw your brother here, and he ran away. And the man was white as a sheet when I came in. He said, Helen, I have to tell you something which is very difficult. But you better run to your mother and aunt and tell them that [SIGHS] he denounced them to the Gestapo. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had been caught. Somebody had denounced him, I think. He had been caught that he had sent illegal money to-- [SIGHS] out of Czechoslovakia under Hitler before we went to camp and all kind of other things. And he had helped my mother, whose sister had left the night Hitler marched in without anything. And she had sent a lot of clothes and jewelry and money and all kinds of stuff illegally out with his help. And when he was denounced and taken to Prague, they told him, if you tell us who else you did those things for, you can go back. If not, you go to Auschwitz. So he gave them my mother and my aunt's name. And by the time I came to my mother's room and my aunt's room, they were already gone. And the Gestapo had picked them up. And there was one building in Terezin in the center where the park was. And that was a German command building. And in the basement, they had bunkers, and that's where they had put them. And [SIGHS] I found out that to go down in order to be able to talk to my mother and see her, I could volunteer to go down and clean in the, in the building. And this way, I also got downstairs and saw her for a few minutes. There was nothing I could do. And then she and her sister were sent to the small fortress, which is in walking distance from Terezin and one of the worst prisons where rarely anybody ever got out alive, which they didn't, either. And the man himself was sent in the next transport to Auschwitz and never had a chance because they used the people to get information. But then they didn't let them live. They immediately tried to do away with them, which, you know, I guess they never thought of that, never believed it and sought to save themselves. And there were, unfortunately, some who did that. And my mother and aunt paid for, for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was-- and they walked to work in the fields. And from one of our army barracks, we could see the street. And so we used to go to the window [BACKGROUND NOISE] and try to see them walk by when they went to work. And so I saw my mother a couple of times and never again. So that was the end of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, after that, I got married in Terezin to a very nice man who came from a small town called Urmitz. And we were married by rabbi. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was in the-- [SIGHS] we had our own, what they called ghetto police. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the ghetto police was really more to keep order amongst us, not to-- you know, they were-- had nothing to do with the Germans. But the only thing that they were strict about was stealing in kitchens or in the bakery or wherever food was used to-- and made, you know, cooked or baked or whatever. And my husband was one of those that of police to, to watch over that. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I worked after that. After I had come back from planting the trees, I worked in the so-called transport commission, which was the office which put the transport to the east together because nobody said Auschwitz. We didn't even know for sure where it's going. It was called transport to the east. And there were certain rules that, you know, the people that worked for the Germans were not allowed to go. The people that we needed to run the ghetto were not allowed to go. So you had to kind of always, you know, try to find people that were not connected to any of that and put them in. And it was a horrible job because, you know, you, you wanted to help one person, and you had to put somebody else in for it. And it's just there had to be 1,000 people ready to go within three days. So we worked day and night. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it got, it got very, very tough. And I got sick for a while and had to-- had something on my lungs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so I wasn't allowed to walk around. And then on top of it, we got a typhoid, typhoid epidemic. And you know, this was an hour and a half away from Prague and in the middle of Czechoslovakia. And the Germans got terribly scared of epidemics anyways.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had marvelous doctors who had come from Prague and Brunn and everything. But we didn't have medication, really. And we didn't have enough to treat them. But when that typhoid epidemic came, then all of a sudden, you know, we got what we needed. And it was really contained and taken care of in, in a marvelous way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that one hospital, they had a park around the building. And there, they had put some cots that people who were like me had to lie there during the day. And they didn't want us to-- you know, other people to catch whatever we had. And while that-- while we were there, they made that famous film where the, the Fuhrer gave a city to the Jews, it was called. And it was made for the Swedish Red Cross. And so I, I worked a little bit on it, too, because we had-- they build a merry-go-round. And I had to find children, because I worked also in the, in the-- taking care of the kitchen of the children's home. And these children had to be trained that they go on the merry-go-round and that the SS man is going to stand there and hand each of the child when it goes by a box of sardines. And they were supposed to say, again, sardines, Uncle Rahm-- was his name, at that time. And on the other end of it, I stood and took them away from them because the whole thing was a sham. They build a bank. They build the stores. They gave us in the hospital sheets [LAUGHS] on the beds. They, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they put flowers on the park in the middle was made, which was bare until then. And then all of a sudden, grass and flowers appeared. Then they had a 5 o'clock band in the afternoon with musicians that were there. And all that was filmed. And the Red Cross people were there and saw everything. And after the war, they said they didn't believe it. But they felt that they didn't want to do anything to make our life worse. So they accepted whatever they were shown. And then one day, [LAUGHS] after all that was very pretty, Mr. Eichmann came on inspection. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had become a very close friend of Mr. Edelstein, who was the head of the Jewish ghetto. And he asked me to come along because when you walk with him, there always has to be one person opening the door and shouting attention wherever we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did that. And it was very strange because I walked ahead of Edelstein and Eichmann and heard them speak Hebrew. I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then later on, Edelstein explained to me that Eichmann had lived in Israel and spoke fluent Hebrew. So he, he had fun talking Hebrew with, with Edelstein. But it was, it was a strange experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, that was the highest ranking SS man that we had ever seen. And his reputation had reached us to, already, that, you know, he's very tough and very hard and very mean and everything else. And then to hear him speak Hebrew and being jolly and laughing and being very friendly with Edelstein was kind of-- I don't know. It was, it was strange. It was scary. And he was scary, too. You just had to look at his eyes, and it was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe his eyes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Eichmann at that time was much younger, of course, than when you saw him on the trial. And he was tall and [BACKGROUND NOISE] slim at that time, still. He gained weight later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't describe anymore. I mean, he was-- as I say, he tried to be-- you know, to have fun with us but in a nasty way, you know? Whatever he said was ironic. And whatever he-- of course, Hebrew, I don't speak. So I didn't understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only know from Edelstein later when he kept saying, well, you Jews look all very well nourished and all very well dressed. And all looks really pretty. And you must have a great life here. But it wasn't-- it was something I remembered. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then came the day when my husband and all the ghetto police were called for the next transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name was Walter Ekstein-- E-K-S-T-E-I-N. Now, when I first started in Terezin-- I forgot to tell you-- and met Edelstein, he put me on his list of 12 people that the SS allowed him to keep and protect till the end of the war in Terezin. He liked me, and he wanted to save me or whatever. And I became good friends with him and his wife and the little boy and spent a lot of time with them, and-- so I was on a list of no transport. But then came Austrians and Germans. And they didn't like-- they wanted to be the-- he was a-- Epstein was the head of the Jewish community in, in Vienna. And [NON-ENGLISH] was another guy who came with him. And they were jealous of Edelstein, who was the most marvelous human being and did a lot for us all. And they denounced him because he covered for some people who run away in the daily count. You know, he had to go every morning and give the count of inmates-- how many men, how many women, how many children, and so on. And he covered up the people who had gotten out. And they denounced him, and so he and his whole family were sent to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he-- when he was gone, I [SIGHS] had no contact anymore with, with the German head because they, they didn't like me either, of course. And there was one lady left whose husband had been sent on the transport with my husband and all the ghetto men. And there were only men. And we were told at that time they went to Germany to build a new ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in order to convince us-- because we always believed everything-- they gave us-- gave all the forms that we used, for whatever was, you know, done and written was forms printed. And they gave those along. They gave baby carriages along for children in that new place. And we believed that they went there. And the next transport came. And the lady whose husband had left her was, before in Terezin, one of the, you know, top men who ran it-- came to me, and I knew her well, and said, she is going to go to join her husband in that new ghetto in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I'll go, too. And I put my name in because I was working there. And I went home to sleep. And I came back, and my name was taken out. And that went on and off till we were already at the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my girlfriend was with me. And she was in the transport and also on her own free will following her husband. And I wasn't allowed to go. I had no number.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the SS man who was standing at the last car and told him that I wanted to go and couldn't. So he opened the door again and said, so it'll be 1,001. And he pushed me in. And that was a regular train. And we looked out of the windows and then suddenly saw that around the window, on the frame of the window, the men had written, Terezin, Auschwitz, and the date-- And the date was?

speaker: interviewee
text: --when they left. So we knew that it wasn't Germany and a new ghetto, but it was Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [SIGHS] sometime since September '44. I really don't know the date. 6? 8? Something-- I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by then, the train was locked, and we left. And sure enough, we landed [LAUGHS] in Auschwitz and not in Germany. What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw my husband again, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen, what did you know about Auschwitz at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, nothing. We really didn't know anything. We knew it was in the east because they always said, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it goes to the east. And the name Auschwitzen was Auschwitz. So we knew it was another camp. But we really didn't know what was going on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of trains were, were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Train-- that was a regular train. You know, plain wooden benches and normal windows, like a third-class, as they called it at that time, train would run, you know. That was still normal train. And then we got out there and saw where we were. And I have a, had a dear friend who was a musician-- was a marvelous pianist. And he played also saxophone, and he played the harmonica. And he had to play in the band-- in the famous band in Auschwitz. Whenever a train arrived, the band came, played.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kurt Mayer. Kurt Mayer used to be a pianist in Prague before the war in a very lovely nightclub there. And I knew him from, you know, from then already. And then, of course, in Terezin, always we saw a lot of him. And, you know, he was one of our group of friends. And he-- I saw him at the station. And he managed, when we walked to Mr. Mengele, and [SIGHS] that is the man who said, left or right, which we didn't know what it means anyway at, at that point. And [SIGHS] he managed afterwards, when I walked on-- and he managed to get to me and asked me-- he told me he was with  my husband and asked me if I should tell him. I said, no, because my husband was happy knowing that I'm protected in Terezin. He didn't know I'm going to join him. He would have been much more unhappy. So I said, no, don't say anything. And that was the last I saw of him till after the war. And then we were in Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which is-- it's very hard to describe. It is so horrible. And I tell you that the smell of the oven is so strong that some years ago I had to go to a doctor to take some cancer spots on my skin away. And he was a very nice old gentleman. And I was, like, was on my back. And I was lying on my back. And he did it with an electric thing. And all of a sudden, I had that smell of burning flesh. I jumped so high that the poor man almost had a heart attack. And I said, I'm terribly sorry, but I didn't know what you were doing. And when I smelled that burning flesh, it just threw me because that was something that it took years to get out of your nose. And so-- and every day in those barracks where we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what--

speaker: interviewee
text: --10, 10 people on one wooden [INAUDIBLE]-- I don't know. It's three tiers on both sides. And in the middle is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a-- how would you describe it? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's not-- it's, it's a-- on one end of it is an, is an oven for the kapo. And the rest is just a, a stone divider of the barrack. And on each [INAUDIBLE] are 10 people-- five and five. And you just lie up there in whatever clothes you have. I got-- after we arrived and they took our clothes, I got one piece of clothes. And you don't get any underwear. You don't get anything except one piece. If you're lucky, it's something you can close. I couldn't because it was a sleeveless little summer dressing gown or whatever without a belt. And that was it. And so you lie there. And once a day in the morning you get a black water, which has nothing in common with coffee. But it has [GERMAN] in it. It has a tranquilizing thing in it. And that's why we were all very, very calm, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: People ask me, how come you had so much, so many people and so few SS women or so that they were not scared that you're going to do something or that, you know? I said, because we were totally phlegmatic almost, you know? And you could taste what was in there. It's-- I don't know, in German it's called [GERMAN]. It's something like a-- it's a, it's a tranquilizer. [SIGHS] And then we got one slice of bread in the evening and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some kind of soup-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do you call it? It wasn't really soup. It was like pea soup, but it wasn't peas. It was some powder. And that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then once a day we had to come down. And the SS would come, and we would go in a circle around them. And they would select. That's what they called the selection. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then once a day, you stood in the morning and then once again in the afternoon for counting. Now, wherever you marched or stood, it was always five in a row because it supposedly was easier for them to count five or 10 and 15. But the only thing was that it never came out the same as it should. And we were standing for hours. And they were counting and recounting. And it was cold, and we had no clothes. And it was horrible. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what happened the day you arrived in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were taken to a barrack where we were told, first of all, undress and throw everything on the floor. And I had a girlfriend with me standing next to me who had a very beautiful light beige coat on. And I kept saying, Ellie, undress. Come on.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, are you crazy? In that, on that dirty floor, I should throw my nice coat? She couldn't, couldn't understand where she is, what it is-- nothing. And I said, just do it because they walk around with hitting you. So we got undressed, and then we were shaved everywhere. And then we, we sat on the [SIGHS] two benches facing each other. And they shaved our head. And they shaved them with a, with a machine. They start in the back, going this way half and then the other half.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, as horrible and as crazy it might sound to you, when you sit in Auschwitz and you look at your girlfriend facing you, all of a sudden with half a head of hair and half nothing, and she, the most beautiful girl, looks horrible at that moment, you start laughing because you don't see yourself. We were all shaved and then got this piece of clothing. And then we were marched to the barracks. And there were puddles because in Auschwitz it's all mud. And it rains a lot, and, you know, it, it, it stays, the puddles. And I know that that I saw a horrible face and, and creature in the thing. And I turned around because I thought it's behind me. But it was me because you have no idea how you look when you are all of a sudden without hair. And so then we were, as I say, in the barracks. And every day we were counted a few times for hours. And then we were-- there was selection. And then we-- the thing was that we had kapos. Those are the-- they were mostly older than we were, longer in camp, and kind of hardened and nasty and vicious women. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had a stick. Each one had some kind of a stick in their hand that that was a sign of their power and of their station, which was above ours. And one day I was standing in the, in the selection [SIGHS] and not knowing really what it is yet, you know. And when they selected girls, the others had to make a, a ring around and hold hands. And they were not allowed to leave. And I was in there-- the last one. And I saw that stick on the floor. And I don't know till today what made me do it. I just bent down, picked it up, and walked out into the circle. And this is how I got out of one selection. And I never got selected again, thank God. But somehow-- I don't know-- guardian angel, I guess. I had so many guardian angels through those years. So I got back into the barracks. And then one day, [SIGHS] we were told to come out. And we were going to a factory, airplane factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was near Dresden in Germany. And there we were taken in cattle cars. And I think we were 75 in one cattle car. So all we could do was stand up. And [SIGHS] it took quite a few days. And we had very little, very little food, just what they gave us on the way-- little pieces of bread and some [SIGHS] rainwater that dripped through the tar roof of those cars. And once they let us out to go to the toilets, which was somewhere in the wilderness. So we ate some grass that was, was there. [SIGHS] And then we went on and came to that little town. Were taken to a building, which they had taken over and where they housed us. And from there, we went to the factory every day, 12, 14 hours.
